coredata.dc:title: Portable dual-function ratio-type triple-emission molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor for the simultaneous visual detection of hepatitis A and B viruses
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Hang
Cai, Ganping
Li, Yong
Jiang, Ning
Chen, Chunyan
Chen, Feng
Cai, Changqun
coredata.dc:description:
Background Viral epidemics have long endangered human health and had dramatic impacts on environment and society. The currently known viruses and the rapid emergence of previously unknown viruses lead to an urgent need for effective virus detection strategies. It is important to develop methods that can detect multiple related viruses simultaneously in order to improve detection efficiency and to avoid treatment delays due to misdiagnoses. However, an important practical problem for viruses simultaneously detection is mutual interference between the targets, which is caused by a certain degree of signal superposition.
Results A portable ratio-based triple-emission molecular imprinting fluorescence sensor was constructed to detect hepatitis A virus (HAV) and hepatitis B virus (HBV) simultaneously, which eliminated background interference and mutual interference between the two targets, and improved detection accuracy. In this sensor, a layer containing blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots (B-CDs) coated with silicon was used as a constant luminescent core to permit ratiometric detection of signals, this layer also affords visual analysis due to its constant blue color. As signal sources to employ yellow-green and red quantum dots, HAV- and HBV-specific molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) were prepared by imprinting virus onto the core containing the B-CDs. Under the optimized detection conditions, the simply physically mixing HAV-MIPs and HBV-MIPs in a specific ratio, which leads to non-interfering fluorescence signals and the simultaneous detection of the two target viruses via the rich color visualization under ultraviolet light.
Significance This strategy provides a simple and low-cost (0.26 ¥/sensor) method for the simultaneous detection of two hepatitis viruses, and provides an avenue for the development of portable methods for the simultaneous detection of similar highly infectious viruses. This self-service sensor also has the potential to be used by patients themselves, reducing pressures on medical staff and decreasing risks of cross-infection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Portable
Simultaneous detection
Visual detection
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Hepatitis A virus (HAV)
Hepatitis B virus (HBV)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of virus MIPs and NIPs
Construction principle of the novel sensor
Optimization of synthesis and detection conditions
Investigating the mechanisms of fluorescence quenching
Linear and visual analyses of the sensors
Analysis of detection selectivity and interference by non-target viruses
Analysis of interference by biological chemicals and the stability of the sensor over time
Analysis of sensors in practical application

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence spectrum and fluorescence color changes of HAV-MIPs and HAV-NIPs detection viruses (From left to right is HAV-NIPs, HAV-NIPs + HAV, HAV-MIPs and HAV-MIPs + HAV in turn); (B) Fluorescence spectrum and fluorescence color changes of HBV-MIPs and HBV-NIPs detected viruses (From left to right is HBV-NIPs, HBV-NIPs + HBV, HBV-MIPs and HBV-MIPs + HBV in turn). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectrum of MIPs detecting viruses (CMIPs = 1.5 mg/mL; mHAV-MIPs:mHBV-MIPs = 1.5:1); (B) NIPs detect the fluorescence spectrum of the virus (CNIPs = 1.5 mg/mL; mHAV-NIPs:mHBV-NIPs = 1.5:1); The inset are changes in color of MIPs and NIPs solutions with HAV and HBV under 365 nm UV irradiation. From left to right is MIPs, MIPs + HAV, MIPs + HBV and MIPs + HAV + HBV in turn (A); NIPs, NIPs + HAV + HBV (B). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) FT-IR spectrum of B-CDs and B-CDs@SiO2. (B) FT-IR spectrum of G-QDs, HAV-MIPs and HAV-NIPs (C) FT-IR spectrum of R-QDs, HBV-MIPs and HBV-NIPs (D) Thermogravimetric analysis diagram of various particles.
Dls and SEM of (A) B-CDs@SiO2, (B) HAV-MIPs, (C) HAV-NIPs, (D) HBV-MIPs, (E) HBV-NIPs.

(A) Different concentrations of HAV (16, 22, 28, 36, 44, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100 nmol/L) virus and different concentrations of HBV (6, 8, 16, 23, 34, 48, 62, 78.5, 88.5, 100 pmol/L) effect on the fluorescence intensity of the sensor (CMIPs = 1.5 mg/mL; mHAV-MIPs:mHBV-MIPs = 1.5:1). (B) Linear relationship of HAV. (C) Linear relationship of HBV. (D) Imaging images under 365 nm ultraviolet light after mixing HAV and HBV at different concentrations. The error bar represents the standard deviation of three parallel measurements.

Selective analysis of sensors for HAV (A) and HBV (B). Binary competitive analysis of sensors for HAV (C) and HBV (D). CMIPs = 1.5 mg/mL; mHAV-MIPs:mHBV-MIPs = 1.5:1, the error bar represents the standard deviation of three parallel determinations.

(A) Anti-interference ability; (B) time stability; (C) repeatability; (D) reproducibility. CMIPs = 1.5 mg/mL; mHAV-MIPs:mHBV-MIPs = 1.5:1, and the concentration of Na+ is 50 μM, the concentration of Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, CO3 2−, HPO4 2− ions as well as the biological compounds l-proline are all 25 μM, the concentration of glucose is 10 μM. The error bar represents the standard deviation of three parallel measurements.
Schematic diagram of the construction of a ratio type triple-emission molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor.
Sensor imprinting factors for HAV and HBV, and selectivity factors for other viruses.
Results of simultaneous detection of HAV and HBV in human serum diluted 100 times.
Comparison between this sensor and other virus sensors.

head.ce:title: Portable dual-function ratio-type triple-emission molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor for the simultaneous visual detection of hepatitis A and B viruses

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yunnan Key Laboratory of Modern Separation Analysis and Substance Transformation
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Viral epidemics have long endangered human health and had dramatic impacts on environment and society. The currently known viruses and the rapid emergence of previously unknown viruses lead to an urgent need for effective virus detection strategies. It is important to develop methods that can detect multiple related viruses simultaneously in order to improve detection efficiency and to avoid treatment delays due to misdiagnoses. However, an important practical problem for viruses simultaneously detection is mutual interference between the targets, which is caused by a certain degree of signal superposition.

A portable ratio-based triple-emission molecular imprinting fluorescence sensor was constructed to detect hepatitis A virus (HAV) and hepatitis B virus (HBV) simultaneously, which eliminated background interference and mutual interference between the two targets, and improved detection accuracy. In this sensor, a layer containing blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots (B-CDs) coated with silicon was used as a constant luminescent core to permit ratiometric detection of signals, this layer also affords visual analysis due to its constant blue color. As signal sources to employ yellow-green and red quantum dots, HAV- and HBV-specific molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) were prepared by imprinting virus onto the core containing the B-CDs. Under the optimized detection conditions, the simply physically mixing HAV-MIPs and HBV-MIPs in a specific ratio, which leads to non-interfering fluorescence signals and the simultaneous detection of the two target viruses via the rich color visualization under ultraviolet light.

This strategy provides a simple and low-cost (0.26 ¥/sensor) method for the simultaneous detection of two hepatitis viruses, and provides an avenue for the development of portable methods for the simultaneous detection of similar highly infectious viruses. This self-service sensor also has the potential to be used by patients themselves, reducing pressures on medical staff and decreasing risks of cross-infection.

A ratio-type triple-emission dual-function MIPs-sensor was successfully constructed to reduce the background interference and mutual interference of target viruses, achieving the simultaneous highly sensitive detection by fluorescence and visual semi-quantitative analysis of HAV and HBV. In particular, the cost of the sensor is rather cheap, 0.03 $ or 0.26 ¥ each.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Viral epidemics, including those involving the novel coronavirus [1], human immunodeficiency virus [2,3], hepatitis virus [4–6] and monkeypox virus [7], have long endangered human health and had dramatic impacts on society. With the rapid emergence of various viruses, effective virus detection strategies are urgently required. To meet this formidable challenge, a variety of virus detection strategies have been developed, including those based on ELISA [8,9], immunofluorescence [10], RT-PCR [11] and nucleic acid sequence-based amplification technologies [12], and many important achievements have been applied to practical detection methods. It is noteworthy that these methods typically focus on detecting a single target virus at a time.

An important practical problem in this realm is the similarity of structure of related viruses, potentially making it difficult to distinguish an epidemic-causing virus within a milieu containing many more similar viruses, such as various types of hepatitis virus and various subtypes of influenza A virus [13]. In addition, co-infection with multiple viruses, such as co-infection with multiple types of hepatitis virus [14] or infections with both human immunodeficiency virus and hepatitis C virus [15], can aggravate a patient's illness and can present an obstacle to virus detection. Therefore, it is important to develop methods that can detect multiple related viruses simultaneously in order to improve detection efficiency and to avoid treatment delays due to misdiagnoses.

Several ingenious methods to detect multiple viruses simultaneously have been proposed. For example, Choi et al. developed a duplex RT-PCR/ELISA assay for the simultaneous detection of two related viruses, hepatitis A virus (HAV) and hepatitis E virus [16]. Chen et al. reported a dual-function fluorescence sensor based on a water-stable three-dimensional Cu-based zwitterionic metal–organic framework that achieved the simultaneous detection of dengue and Zika virus RNA sequences [17]. Zhang et al. reported a digital single virus immunoassay based on fluorescent magnetic multifunctional nanospheres and applied it to the ultra-sensitive detection of multiple avian influenza viruses [18]. While these strategies are effective, they typically require costly and unstable biological agents, such as RNA probes or antibodies, and they also must be administered by highly skilled technicians using complicated instruments over long detection times. Most importantly, mutual interference between the targets in actual detection, which is caused by a certain degree of signal superposition, and background interference was difficult to avoid in the above-mentioned strategies.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are known as “artificial antibodies”, which have the advantages of high selectivity, easy preparation and stability [19–21]. With the development of sustainable chemistry, the development of sustainable MIPs has attracted great interest [22–24]. In 2020, fourteen principles of green molecular imprinting were proposed by Chen et al., which provided a framework for the preparation of sustainable MIPs [25,26]. Based on the concept of sustainable MIPs, various MIPs sensors have been developed to specifically identify and detect small molecules, such as amino acids and drugs [27–30] and biological macromolecules, such as proteins [31–33], viruses [34–36] and bacteria [37,38]. In the development of more practical virus simultaneous detection strategies, MIPs fluorescent sensors have presented multiple important advantages [39,40]. However, due to the complex structure and huge size of viruses, they are usually more likely to interfere with each other. Along with the research, a tricolor emission MIPs sensor was found, which can further improve the detection accuracy and reduce the detection error of the sensor through self-calibration. At the same time, different fluorescent color windows provide rich color changes, which is convenient for visual inspection [41–44]. For example, Yang et al. constructed a tricolor emission fluorescence MIPs sensor by post-imprinting mixed blue-, green-, and red-based MIPs in an optimized ratio for the multiplexed and visual detection of bovine hemoglobin [43]. Chen et al. constructed a dual-nanozyme cascade catalysis triple-emission fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor for simultaneous detection of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid and 2, 4-dichlorophenol [44]. In this case, we anticipated that we could provide constant luminescence by introducing the third quantum dots (QDs), allowing ratio determination, thus reducing the mutual interference of the fluorescence signals of the two viruses, eliminating the background fluorescence signal and improving the accuracy and reliability of detection. As far as we know, there is no report in the literature on the simultaneous detection of viruses by MIP-QDs tricolor emission fluorescence sensors.

Specifically, in this sensor, silicon spheres coated with blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots (B-CDs) were used as the imprinted core carriers, and green yellow cadmium telluride quantum dots (G-QDs) and red cadmium telluride quantum dots (R-QDs) were used as signal sources to prepare HAV-MIPs and HBV-MIPs, respectively. The mixing of these two MIPs in a controlled proportion and concentration led to the construction of a ratio-type triple-emission dual-function MIPs-sensor that achieved the highly sensitive simultaneous fluorescent detection of HAV and HBV as well as a semi-quantitative visual analysis. The B-CDs worked as a light source that permitted the ratiometric detection, but it also acted as a blue background that emphasized the changes to the colors of the MIPs under UV light, leading to an unambiguous visualization. The development of visual detection methods is important when working with highly contagious viruses, as the risk of infection can be completely avoided by the establishment of a self-service testing strategy that can be performed by the patient. Notably, this strategy also reduces the cost of detection to 0.03 $ or 0.26 ¥ per sensor. Please see the detailed accounting process (Table S3 in Supplementary Material).

Tetraethyl silicate (TEOS), cadmium chloride hydrate (CdCl2·2.5H2O) and polyethylene imine (PEI) were provided by Shanghai Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd; Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) provided by Hubei Dongcao Chemical Technology Co., Ltd; 3-Ureidopropyltriethoxysilane (UPTES), tellurium provided by Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd; Ammonia water and EtOH provided by Hunan Huihong Reagent Co., Ltd; MeOH was provided by Tianjin Hengxing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd; Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was provided by Shanghai Saen Co., Ltd; Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), citric acid (CA) and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) were provided by Shanghai Bide Pharmaceutical Technology Co., Ltd.; HAV was provided by Zhejiang Pukang Biotechnology Co., Ltd. and HBV was provided by Shenzhen Kangtai Biological Products Co., Ltd. All the reagents were analytically pure and were not further purified before use.

Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific), Scanning Electron Microscope (JEOL Corporation of Japan), Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Hitachi Technology Co., Ltd), Comprehensive Thermal Analyzer (TA Company of the United States), Portable ultraviolet analyzer (Lichen Technology Co., Ltd), Drying Box (Hunan Honglin Instrument Co., Ltd), Zeta potential particle size meter (Marvin Instruments UK Limited).

B-CDs were synthesized by using CA as a carbon source and PEI as a nitrogen source [45,46]. CA (0.5 g) and PEI (0.1 g) were ultrasonically dissolved in ultra-pure water (8 mL). The solution was transferred to a PTFE reactor and kept at 120 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, the solution was allowed to naturally cool to room temperature. Methanol (8 mL) was added, and the mixture was centrifuged at 12,300 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was discarded, and the collected carbon quantum dots (CDs) were dispersed in 4 mL ultra-pure water. These samples were stored between 2 and 8 °C. Ultra-pure water (5 mL) and ethanol (95 mL) were added to the samples of B-CDs stock solution (500 μL). The B-CDs were dispersed to uniformity by ultrasound, and the sample was stirred mechanically at 700 rpm with the slow addition of TEOS (2000 μL) and ammonia (2000 μL). The mixture was allowed to react with stirring for 12 h. After the reaction, the solid was collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm and washed with ethanol three times. The collected solid was dried in vacuum at 60 °C, and B-CDs@SiO2 was obtained.

Based on the previous literature [47], G-QDs and R-QDs were synthesized. First, synthesis of NaHTe precursor: Mix anhydrous ethanol (2 mL) and ultra-pure water (2 mL), introduce argon gas to pre bubble deoxidize, add tellurium powder (76.6 mg) and NaBH4 (80 mg) to a mixed solution of predeoxidized ultra-pure water and anhydrous ethanol, deoxidize the device, and react and stir under argon atmosphere in an ice water bath at 0 °C for 4 h to obtain a pink precursor solution, which is used in a timely manner.

In addition, dissolve CdCl2·2.5H2O (184.8 mg) in ultra-pure water (150 mL), add MPA (126 μL), and disperse uniformly by ultrasound. After that, the pH of the above solution was adjusted to 9–10 using sodium hydroxide solution (1 mol/L). Then, the solution is injected into argon and bubbled for 60 min to remove oxygen, and a water pump was used to deoxygenate until no bubbles were generated. Under argon atmosphere, 2 mL of NaHTe precursor was injected at 100 °C, followed by magnetic stirring, condensation and reflux for 2 h, and G-QDs was obtained. In the preparation of R-QDs, it is sufficient to extend the condensation reflux time. The obtained QDs were sealed and stored in a cool place.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of virus MIPs and NIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Dried B-CDs@SiO2 (20 mg) was ultrasonically dispersed in PBS (10 mL, 0.01 M, pH 7.4). Then, APTES (25 μL for HAV and 20 μL for HBV), UPTES (20 μL for HAV, 15 μL for HBV), HAV (50 μL, 80.3 μmol/L) or HBV (50 μL, 8.34 nmol/L) stock solution and 3 mL of G-QDs (for HAV) or R-QDs (for HBV) were added. The reaction was stirred at 800 rpm at room temperature for 3 h. Next, TEOS (40 μL) and NH3·H2O (40 μL) were slowly added, and stirring was continued for 8 h for HAV-MIPs and 9 h for HBV-MIPs. After the reaction, the particles were collected by centrifugation and eluted with an ethanol: acetonitrile solution (9:1, v/v) for 5 times. Finally, the particles were eluted 3 times with ultrapure water, and then dried under a vacuum at 50 °C to obtain HAV-MIPs or HBV-MIPs particles. Non-imprinted particles (HAV-NIPs and HBV-NIPs) were preparated in the same way except that PBS was used instead of the template virus. Store the obtained particles in a sealed container and keep them in a dry and cool place.

HAV-MIPs and HBV-MIPs particles, at a ratio of 1.5:1, were dispersed ultrasonically in PBS, and 1 mL of a detection solution with a total concentration of MIPs of 1.5 mg/mL was prepared. The fluorescence intensity of the solution (λex = 370 nm; λem = 445 nm, 564 nm, or 663 nm; slit width = 5 nm) was recorded as F0. Add samples containing known concentrations of HAV and HBV, and incubate the mixture for 2 h under optimized detection conditions. After this incubation, the fluorescence intensity of the solution was recorded as Fi; thus, the change of the fluorescence intensity due to the addition of the virus was calculated as ΔFF0 - Fi.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction principle of the novel sensor
ce:section.ce:para: A schematic diagram showing the principles underlying the ratio-based triple-emission molecular imprinted fluorescence sensor for simultaneous detection of HAV and HBV is shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, B-CDs, G-QDs, and R-QDs were synthesized. These reagents exhibit different color representations with no interference at their respective emission wavelengths when excited at a single wavelength (370 nm). CDs in particular have been increasingly applied to microbial sensing and biological imaging due to their high optical stability, high optical absorption, and chemical stability [48,49]. Therefore, in the present study, B-CDs were coated with silicon layers as imprinted carrier materials and the luminescent core of a constant ratio fluorescence signal source in order to reduce background signals and to reduce the mutual interference of the two target viruses, and thus to improve detection accuracy.

Subsequently, two silylation reagents, APTES and UPTES, were added as functional monomers. Amino-modified silica spheres were used to immobilize the virus particles, and QDs were introduced. TEOS was used as a crosslinking agent, and NH3·H2O was used as an initiator to embed the virus into the polymeric network layer. The combination of multiple functional silylation monomers was used in order to maximize the number of virus binding sites in order to improve the accuracy of detection and viral capture, while reducing nonspecific adsorption and mutual interference in samples in which both viruses are present [50–53]. After the template virus was eluted, imprinted cavities matching the shape and size of HAV and HBV were left as capture sites in the imprinted layers of the two MIPs. The nature of these sites then permitted the specific identification and capture of HAV and HBV from test samples, which would cause the quenching of the fluorescence of the corresponding QDs. Then, by simply physically mixing HAV-MIPs and HBV-MIPs in an optimized ratio and concentration, the ratio-type triple-emission MIPs fluorescence sensor was constructed to detect HAV and HBV. The B-CDs background also provided a very good detectable visualization effect.

The feasibility of the proposed sensor was verified by application to test samples of HAV and HBV particles. As shown in Fig. 1 , when HAV or HBV was added, the fluorescence intensity of the corresponding HAV-MIPs or HBV-MIPs decreased significantly, while the fluorescence intensity of the control polymers HAV-NIPs and HBV-NIPs remained essentially unchanged. These results indicated that the cavities imprinted by the viruses specifically recognize the corresponding virus, and the material itself does not exhibit a strong non-specific adsorption. It should also be noted that when visualized with a 365 nm UV lamp, the addition of the target virus to the relevant viral MIPs also results in a detectable color change (the illustration in Fig. 1); this phenomenon demonstrates the feasibility of visual detection using the constructed sensor.

The highly specific detection of the target virus in the presence of HAV-MIPs or HBV-MIPs alone was achieved, which provides a basic guarantee for achieving simultaneous detection of HAV and HBV. But we also investigated the simultaneous detection of the two viruses. Here, HAV-MIPs and HBV-MIPs were mixed to produce the MIPs sample, while HAV-NIPs and HBV-NIPs were mixed to produce the NIPs control sample. As shown in Fig. 2 , when HAV was added to the MIPs, only the MIPs fluorescence intensity at the HAV-specific emission wavelength (564 nm) changed; similarly, when HBV was added, only the fluorescence intensity at the HBV-specific emission wavelength (663 nm) changed. When both HAV and HBV were added, however, the fluorescence at both 564 nm and 663 nm were quenched (Fig. 2A). In the case of NIPs, adding either a single virus or both viruses did not lead to quenching (Fig. 2B). These results indicate that the constructed MIPs sensor can achieve specific simultaneous detection of HAV and HBV, and the two signal outputs do not interfere with each other.

The illustration in Fig. 2A is the result of visualization under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. It can be seen that with the addition of different viruses, the colors of the NIPs control sensors did not change. The MIPs sensor exhibited a purplish-red color upon addition of HAV due to the partial quench of G-QDs, and a light orange color was observed after the addition of HBV due to the partial quench of R-QDs. When HAV and HBV were added together, the two QDs were partially quenched, and the MIPs sensor was a light pink color. These confirmatory experiments show that the proposed MIPs sensor can achieve high selectivity recognition and sensitive naked-eye visual detection.

Next, in order to confirm the synthesis and to investigate the properties of the synthesized polymers, we used Fourier transform spectroscopy (FT-IR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), and comprehensive thermogravimetric analysis to characterize the properties, sizes, and morphologies of B-CDs, HAV-MIPs, HAV-NIPs, HBV-MIPs, and HBV-NIPs. In the FT-IR spectra shown in Fig. 3 A, the characteristic peaks at 1060 cm−1 and 1720 cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetric tensile vibrations from the C–NH–C of the B-CDs and the characteristic peaks at –CONH– [48], respectively. The bending vibrations at 469 cm−1, symmetrical stretching at 798 cm−1 and the antisymmetric telescopic vibrations at 1100 cm−1 were assigned to Si–O–Si, indicating the successful preparation and coating of B-CDs by silicon layers.

In the QDs, mercaptopropionic acid exists in its ionic form, and the absorption peak at 1640 cm−1 was assigned to the QDs (Fig. 3B and C). The peaks at 791 cm−1 and 1090 cm−1, respectively, indicate tensile vibrations of Si–O and Si–O–Si. Notably, both MIPs and NIPs have the same characteristic peaks, suggesting that the imprinted particles have the same properties as the non-imprinted materials.

The thermal weight loss of various particles from 0 to 800 °C was analyzed (Fig. 3D). In these analyses, changes observed over the range of 0–100 °C are mainly due to the loss of water from the sample. In the present analysis, the MIPs exhibited the most rapid weight loss, followed by the NIPs and then B-CDs@SiO2. It can be found that the final weight loss rate is MIPs > NIPs > B-CDs@SiO2, this is because template viruses are introduced into MIPs, leaving a few viruses embedded in the innermost part. As an imprinted core, the composition of B-CDs@SiO2 is relatively simple, so its weight loss rate is the lowest. Taken together, these characteristics indicate the successful preparation of the desired imprinted polymers.

The characterization of the water contact angles (Fig. S1) showed that each particle exhibits hydrophilicity. Topographical maps of each particle were constructed using SEM (Fig. 4 ). Here, the surface of B-CDs@SiO2 was observed to be smooth, and the particle presented a uniformly dispersed spherical shape (Fig. 4A). Upon the introduction of the imprinted layer, the morphologies of HAV-MIPs and HBV-MIPs became rough, and the particle sizes increased, the MIPs and NIPs were of similar size and shape (Fig. 4B–D). The sizes of various particles were measured by DLS (Fig. 4). The particle sizes of B-CDs@SiO2, HAV-MIPs, HAV-NIPs, HBV-MIPs and HBV-NIPs were about 246.0 nm, 287.2 nm, 290.4 nm, 300.5 nm and 301.6 nm, respectively. The increase in size upon the addition of an imprinted layer was as expected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis and detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to optimize the results, several factors that would potentially influence the synthesis and detection were investigated. Detailed are shown in Supporting Information. The optimization results of the synthesized HAV-MIPs are shown in Table S1, and the results indicated that the optimal dosage of TEOS, APTES, and UPTES are 40 μL, 25 μL and 20 μL in turn. Table S2 shows that the optimal dosage of TEOS, APTES, and UPTES for synthesizing HBV-MIPs is 40 μL, 20 μL and 15 μL in turn. The synthesis time of the two MIPs was optimized, and the results were evaluated by the IF of MIPs (Fig. S2). The results show that the optimal synthesis times for HAV-MIPs and HBV-MIPs are 8 h and 9 h, respectively.

For the detection step, the optimal dosage ratio of two polymers of HAV-MIPs: HBV-MIPs is 1.5:1 (Fig. S3). The optimal concentration of the two MIPs is 1.5 mg/mL (Fig. S4), the optimal pH value is 7.4 (Fig. S5), the incubation time on detection of HAV and HBV are all 80 min (Fig. S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating the mechanisms of fluorescence quenching
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the principles leading to the signal output of the sensor, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the QDs was studied. Fluorescence quenching mechanisms can be divided into two categories: static quenching and dynamic quenching. Static quenching can be distinguished from dynamic quenching due to the fact that during static quenching, the UV absorption spectrum of fluorescent substances changes with the formation of ground state complexes by fluorescent groups and quenching groups. In the sensor described here, two types of QDs serve as fluorescent groups, and the corresponding viruses serve as quenching groups. It was found that the presence of the virus did not alter the UV absorption of the fluorescent group (Fig. S7A); therefore, we concluded that fluorescence quenching occurs via a dynamic quenching mechanism.

Dynamic quenching can be categorized as fluorescence energy resonance transfer and charge transfer. These types can be distinguished by exploring whether the UV absorption spectra of the quenching group (here, the virus particles) overlaps with the fluorescence emission spectra the fluorophore (here, the MIPs). If the spectra do overlap, it is likely that dynamic quenching occurs via fluorescence energy resonance transfer. In the present case, the relevant spectra do not overlap (Fig. S7B), suggesting that quenching occurs via a charge transfer mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear and visual analyses of the sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimized conditions, different concentrations of HAV and HBV were added to the prepared sensors, and the sensor performance was evaluated (Fig. 5 ). It was found that the fluorescence intensity values at emission wavelengths 564 nm and 663 nm decreased with increasing target virus concentration over the concentration ranges of 16–100 nM (HAV) and 6–100 pM (HBV), while the fluorescence at 445 nm remained essentially unchanged. The ratios of each signal (fluorescence intensity at λem = 564 or 663 nm) to the fluorescence intensity at λem = 445 nm were determined as functions of virus concentration, and linear regression equations were obtained. For HAV, the linear range of detection was determined to be between 16.0 nM and 90.0 nM, and the linear equation over this range was (F564/F445)0/(F564/F445)i = 0.02876 × CHAV + 0.55296, R2 = 0.99906, limit of detection (LOD) is 3.55 nM (Fig. 5B). For HBV, the linear range of detection was determined to be between 6.0 and 88.5 pM, and the linear equation was (F663/F445)0/(F663/F445)i = 0.03200 × CHBV + 0.7947, R2 = 0.99346, LOD is 1.97 pM (Fig. 5C). Because these linear equations were determined in the presence of both viruses, the strong linear correlations indicate that the sensor signal is quantitative when simultaneously detecting HAV and HBV, and the two fluorescent signals do not interfere with each other, leading to excellent detection performance.

Fig. 5D shows a visual image of a sensor incubated with either HAV or HBV alone or at different concentrations simultaneously followed by illumination under a 365 nm UV lamp. The 25 holes in the figure represent vertical and horizontal intersections (5 × 5) of 25 combinations of concentrations; the horizontal direction from left to right represents the increasing HAV concentrations (0, 22, 44, 70, and 90 nM), and the vertical direction from top to bottom represents increasing HBV concentrations (0, 8, 34, 62, and 88.5 pM). In this figure, the intensity of the red color can be seen to increase with an increasing concentration of HAV (horizontal rows), while the intensity of the green color can similarly be seen to increase with an increasing concentration of HBV (vertical rows). This figure demonstrates the effectiveness of visual detection using the proposed sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of detection selectivity and interference by non-target viruses
ce:section.ce:para: To comprehensively evaluate the selectivity of the constructed sensor to HAV, which form icosahedrons with diameters of approximately about 27 nm, and HBV, which form spherical structures of approximately 42 nm in diameter, various similar viruses were selected for comparison (Fig. 6 ). These viruses included enterovirus 71 (EV71) (spherical, 20–30 nm in diameter) [54], rabies viruses (RV; warhead shape, with a cross-sectional diameter of approximately 75 nm and a length of approximately 180 nm) [55], and H5N1 virus (elliptical, 80–120 nm in diameter). The experiments were conducted by incubating the sensor with identical concentrations of these target and non-target viruses. When the sensor was used for the detection of non-target viruses, the fluorescence signal output remained essentially unchanged (Fig. 6A and B). Similarly, essentially no interference was observed with non-target viruses included in attempting to detect target viruses (Fig. 6C and D).

Recognition by MIPs mainly depends on the shape of the imprinted sites and the functional groups in the imprinted cavity; therefore, the shape and complementary functional groups of interacting particles is important. The spherical shape of EV71 is similar to that of both HAV and HBV; the observed lack of interaction with the MIPs in this study suggests, then, that different surface structures and surface properties keep the spherical EV71 particles from matching imprinted sites. RV has a distinctly different shape from that of the template viruses, making it unable to enter the imprinting cavity and to be recognized by the MIPs. H5N1 has a much larger diameter than either HAV or HBV; this size difference and differences in functional groups must prevent it from accurately matching the imprinted sites. Therefore, we conclude that only the precise combinations of HAV and HAV-MIPs or HBV and HBV-MIPs lead to changes in the fluorescence signals. In other words, we determined that the proposed sensor recognizes the target viruses with strong selectivity, and the signal is not inhibited in the presence of other viruses.

The imprinting factor (IF) of each target virus was calculated by the formula IFHAV = ΔHAV-MIPs/ΔHAV-NIPs and IFHBV = ΔHBV-MIPs/ΔHBV-NIPs, where ΔHAV-MIPs = [(F564/F445)0/(F564/F445)]HAV-MIPs, ΔHAV-NIPs = [(F564/F445)0/(F564/F445)]HAV-NIPs, ΔHBV-MIPs = [(F663/F445)0/(F663/F445)]HBV-MIPs, ΔHBV-NIPs = [(F663/F445)0/(F663/F445)]HBV-NIPs. The selectivity factor (SF) of potentially competing analytes was calculated by the formula SF = IFtarget/IFnon-target (Table 1 ). It is noteworthy that the constructed sensor achieved satisfactory selectivity when detecting two target viruses simultaneously.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of interference by biological chemicals and the stability of the sensor over time
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the ability of the constructed sensor to detect viruses under actual conditions, several potentially interfering compounds from biological matrixes were added to the detection system. Specifically, we explored interference by Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, CO3 2−, HPO4 2− ions as well as the biological compounds glucose and l-proline. When these substances were included in the process, the detection of HAV and HBV by the sensor was not significantly affected (Fig. 7 A), and the system retained high specificities for the targets. These results indicated that detection by the sensor is not interfered with by common biological chemicals and suggested that the sensor would maintain stability upon application of real samples.

The stability of the sensor over time was also investigated (Fig. 7B). Here, single batches of the synthesized materials were incubated for up to 5 weeks at room temperature before application to viral detection. It was found that after 5 weeks, the signals from the proposed sensor were only slightly lower than initial values (81.4 % for HAV and 82.2 % for HBV), indicating satisfactory stability.

To evaluate the ability to repeatedly use the sensor, the binding and elution of target virus from the sensor were performed over multiple cycles. It was found that after five such cycles, the efficiencies of detection of HAV and HBV were 82.0 % and 80.5 %, respectively, indicating that the imprinting layer of the proposed sensor was not damaged to a large extent during repeated elution, and the impression chamber remained reasonably complete. These results suggest that the sensor can be reused multiple times (Fig. 7C).

To evaluate the reproducibility of detection results, MIPs synthesized in five different batches were used for the detection of HAV and HBV. It was found that the signal outputs of the batches were almost identical, indicating that the accuracy of detection by the proposed sensors are highly reproducible (Fig. 7D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of sensors in practical application
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of the sensor to the identification of viruses in actual blood samples, the sensor was used to detect HAV and HBV particles in human serum that had been diluted 100-fold (Table 2 ). The recovery rates of both viruses were compared to concentrations of virus spiked into the samples, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were determined upon conducting the experiments in triplicate. The recovery rates were determined to be between 101.1 and 113.0 % for HAV and between 97.15 and 104.1 % for HBV, indicating that the constructed sensor can be applied in practical situations.

The developed MIPs-sensor was compared with other sensors for detecting viruses in terms of linear range and LOD as well as the ability to detect multiple viruses simultaneously and to provide a visual output (Table 3 ). According to a literature review, most of the current viral detection methods have only been tested in the context of individual detection, and most applications are thus relatively narrow [56,57]. In the field of simultaneous detection, a fluorescent sensor based on metal-organic framework materials have been reported; specifically, this sensor realized the simultaneous detection of the RNA sequences of Zika and dengue viruses [17]. However, biological agents, such as RNA probes or antibodies are required, which leads to the need for complicated and expensive extraction steps, and this sensor also did not achieve semi-quantitative visual detection. Recently, our group has also made unremitting efforts in this field. For example, a highly selective dual-function MIPs sensor was constructed. The SF was as high as 13.7, but it failed to realize visual detection [39]. Later, the simultaneous visual detection of two viruses based on two different QDs was developed by our group [40]. Based on the previous works, a dual-function ratio type triple-emission MIPs-sensor is proposed, which is beneficial to eliminate background signal and reduce mutual interference of two target viruses, leading to improve detection accuracy. This sensor achieves highly specific recognition and semi-quantitative visual detection of HAV and HBV, and it achieves a detection range and sensitivity comparable to that of a single-function MIPs sensor.

In summary, a ratio-based triple-emission MIPs portable fluorescence sensor for the simultaneous detection of HAV and HBV was developed. HAV and HBV were separately imprinted on the outer layer of a core containing B-CDs of constant luminescence, and fluorescent cadmium telluride quantum dots with two different emission positions were introduced into HAV-MIPs and HBV-MIPs as signal outputs. Upon mixing HAV-MIPs and HBV-MIPs in an optimized ratio and concentration, the sensor was found to detect HAV and HBV simultaneously with reduced background interference and a low mutual interference between the two targets. In particular, under UV light, the sensitive visual semi-quantitative analysis of targets was achieved as a rich color change. The constructed sensor has satisfactory selectivity, stability, ability to work in the presence of biological chemicals, repeatability, and reproducibility, and it exhibited an excellent recovery rate in serum, indicating a good potential for practical application. This strategy is expected to serve as an inexpensive multi-modal universal platform for the simultaneous detection of similar highly infectious viruses. This portable sensor also has the potential to be used by patients themselves, reducing pressures on medical staff and decreasing risks of cross-infection.

Hang Gong: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization. Ganping Cai: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yong Li: Investigation, Conceptualization. Ning Jiang: Conceptualization. Chunyan Chen: Data curation, Conceptualization. Feng Chen: Conceptualization. Changqun Cai: Validation, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775132), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (No. 2022JJ30557), Postgraduate Scientific Research Innovation Project of Hunan Province (CX20210639, QL20220148), Postgraduate Scientific Research Innovation Project of Xiangtan University (XDCX2021B163, XDCX20220148).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2024.343451.
